speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape number five, and the survivor's Mr. Solly Irving. Before the tape break, you were just talking about your experiences in Latrun. Can I just ask, did you have any numbers assigned to you in the army?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, of course. The army-- each one had his-- everybody has a number. My number, if I still remember, it was 58252. [INAUDIBLE] Yes-- [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: And also, I forgot to mention, but when you were in the camps, um, in the four different camps that you were in, did you have any numbers assigned--

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember, as such, but it must have been. It must have been, because normally, they used to call you up with numbers-- never names. Although there are names of, uh, each--the German's were so, um, everything so precise, that they've got names of the many camps-- those that, uh, they've kept-- uh, the names [INAUDIBLE]. But I would imagine I must have had a number, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then to move on, when you Latrun, what happened from there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well it was the uh-- May came up, towards May then. And, uh, it was a ceasefire. And we were getting ourselves ready to, uh, to have the parade-- the first parade ever. We were given insignias then-- because [NON-ENGLISH] never had insignias. [NON-ENGLISH] was mostly of a kibbutzim-- members of kibbutzim.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we were given insignias, then, um, which I got my first one. And we started brushing ourselves up. Uh, I was then taught how to make white belts-- if you put salt on it, and you leave it in the sun, and you rub it hard and have elbow grease, you get white belts.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we polished ourselves up, and we were assembled in Tel Aviv. And it's called the [NON-ENGLISH]-- in other words, the parade which never took place. There was such commotions, such disorganization-- they had to call it off.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which parade was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was the first independence parade-- Israeli independence parade.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what date was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in May. Um, May, um can't remember the exact date. It was May.

speaker: interviewee
text: 1949, May of '49, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then, after the army--

speaker: interviewee
text: After the army, I started going into town-- Tel Aviv-- and met the-- we had a, it was a club called the Anglo-Saxon club, near [? Con ?] Street, and [? Allenbee ?] Street. And I met some of the boys from our lot-- the [INAUDIBLE] Society. And, uh, some of them were talking about going back. I, uh, applied for the mobilization, [? they ?] were to go back. But before that, I stayed with my aunt-- even when I had leave, I'd stay with my aunt in Tel Aviv, who was remarried then to a very, very orthodox man-- a [NON-ENGLISH]. Even those days, they had no food the whole week, but Shabbat, the table was laid like for a king. I don't know what my aunt was doing, but he was working in a candle factory. So he couldn't have been earning very much. Anyway he took a dislike to me. I wasn't as religious as I should have been, maybe. He couldn't see the fun of leaving [? Gateshead-- ?] he knew what [? Gateshead was-- ?] to come to Israel. He didn't approve of the army, and so-- And he was a man who survived the wars. So we didn't get on, and I didn't stay with him. I went to stay with my uncle. And the story of how I met my uncle, and how my uncle met me-- he was also in the army. I went looking for him-- I couldn't find him. He wasn't home. I went to his house-- where he lived, I stayed there a while. And I came across my-- I stopped some girl on a bike, on a bicycle, and asked for Emily [? Finkelman. ?] And she already heard that I wasn't in Israel. She threw down the bike, and started shouting in Hebrew-- [SPEAKING HEBREW]. That means me-- Solly has arrived. Little Solly is alive. And shouting and screaming and [INAUDIBLE]. Anyway, I stayed in it. He came looking for me, into a camp. And that particular camp, I was sort of, um, a guard, a policeman in the gate. And, uh, he came ask me if I knew me-- and we both embraced each other. That was-- anyway, I went to live with him. And he wanted me to come live with him-- that's the beginning. He wanted me to have an education, he would look after me. And I looked this sort of-- the way they, uh, lived. However small that they had a place, they still had a young family sharing it, so they found a little gap for me to put my head down. And things were very, very hard-- he was working hard. I don't know how they made ends meet, and I couldn't deny the opportunity to come back to England, and I couldn't see myself being under a family-- I wasn't used to having a family. As much as I wanted-- I loved him, dearly. But I decided to come back. We also-- we were told that we wouldn't be able to stay on in England. We were only displaced persons-- our pass was only valid for a year. So we had to come back within the year. And those unfortunate who threw their passports away into the-- in the sea, I say, I want to keep it as a souvenir. And I kept it. And that's how I was able to come back. Once it was still valid, even for valid for an hour, they had to let us in and give us an extension-- two months, three months. And that's what I got. And I thought, well, we'll have a holiday-- and we then, we'll go back. It didn't cost us to come back to England. But unfortunate, when we left Israel, it was already Israel. And the government insisted on giving us an exit visa. So when I arrived, first of all we stopped off in Paris-- we got to Paris, and we were told that they had to let us-- let us in. And we mustn't disclose what we were doing, because they will ask us. Because our visas previously was for Switzerland, which I never reached. Anyway, we got to Dieppe, New Haven, and I got to be one of the first off the boat. And we got to immigration, the guy said, ah, he said, another one. Take a seat. We've been waiting for people like you. OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: We weren't-- I wasn't frightened or anything-- all they could do is not let me in. But I would have gone back to Paris, and-- and through there. Anyway, after a long time, one train goes, another train goes. And I went over and I said, look, all the trains left already. How am I going to get back to London?

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, don't worry, we'll put a special train on to you-- for you. And they started interrogating me, eventually.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was I doing? Where have I been? How did I go to-- to Palestine, Israel? And all the stories, we already sorted out. And he said, well, no wonder your uncle didn't want to keep you-- because you weren't allowed to work. So therefore, what-- what were you doing here? You couldn't afford to keep you, so you came back. I say, yeah, quite right. Huh, OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And eventually I got, uh, the extension, and the CBF-- [NON-ENGLISH]-- extended and extended it, and I managed to, uh-- and must have been '51, '52-- '51, I think. A friend of mine was getting married in Brussels-- also two refugees. He was at 45, [NON-ENGLISH]. And she had family in Brussels. So they decided to have their wedding in Brussels.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I wanted to go up. Another friend of mine who lived in [? Canada ?] wanted to go-- I saved money. And so I went and applied my-- my travel document had expired. So I applied for a travel to-- [INAUDIBLE] at the home office, which was in [? Holven. ?] Got a note back to say, would I come out to the office? I went out. And sitting across the desk-- he had the little windows-- and the guy said to me, I see your application, and will you please tell me the story where you have been that 1948 to '49? So I said, surely you don't want me to repeat every word of what happened? If I make a mistake, you'll pull me up on it. And he looked at me in the eyes, and he said, I'm sure if you put your mind to it, not one word will be different.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that's what it was. He gave me an extension and I stayed on, started in business eventually, applied for naturalization papers and got it. And I've been here ever since.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when were you-- and when were you made a British citizen?

speaker: interviewee
text: Must have been-- I can't remember the exact date. I was running business-- it must have been around about '55, '56.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what business were you in?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was in the handbag manufacturing. When I came back from Israel, I went to see a friend of mine who was working in the handbags in Stamford Hill, in [INAUDIBLE]. And but originally, I wanted to be an [INAUDIBLE] engineer, or an electrical engineer. But the apprenticeship was only paid 1 pounds 50-- one and a half pounds.

speaker: interviewer
text: Board and lodgings, I wasn't supported anywhere-- had to make my own way. But the cheapest was 3 pounds. I wasn't a great mathematician, but I couldn't-- couldn't figure it out, how, what, when. So when I saw this guy, he said, well, you got a job to go to? So I said, no, I haven't.

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, you can start here for 3 pounds a week. I didn't know anything about handbags, or anything. But started off, and within a year, I left that place-- not even a year-- and applied as a-- an experienced man for 7 pound a week, and got the job. I didn't have any tools, did nothing. And when I would apply for the job, there were three of us applying for the same thing. And in those days, certain material was so hard and heavy-- like steel-- you had to open wide to get-- to fit the things into the, uh, what we called the frames. And there was two other guys started the same day. Whatever they were doing-- long bench-- it was [? Huyton Handbags ?] in Liverpool Street. [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: And I went and whatever they were doing, I was copying. And he said to me, where are your tools? You're an experienced man? I says, well, I didn't want to take the other plat-- I didn't want them to know that I was looking for another job. And I went, and this was a Monday morning, on Wednesday he called me-- the others left. It was too hard for them.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he said to me, you not experienced, but I admire your guts. And I finished up being manager there. After three years, I left and I started my own. '49, '52-- and in '52 I started my own.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did you meet your late wife?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, my late wife, I met '60, '61.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Got married July 15, 1962.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sandra Ann Silver. She was English born-- her parents lived in South London. Unfortunately, her mother died 10 months ago. She died on June 29, 1971-- a young girl of 29. My old-- my youngest daughter, she had-- we had two children, we got two children-- my youngest one was five-- only five years old. Who's now married, got her own family. My oldest one, Hazel-- Hazel [? Rumer-- ?] is 33, 34 coming up. She's not married-- she hasn't found her right partner yet, please God. Hope in my time, still, that she'll find the right partner. So I was-- everybody-- it was [? pain's ?] ambition to find-- the charge and find their own happiness. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: What's your youngest daughter's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ruth Miral.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your grandchildren?

speaker: interviewee
text: Grandchild one is Scott Pinney-- Pincus-Chaim. Pinney after his great grandfather, who was Rabbi Shepson-- a well-known rabbi. Chaim because my late wife's name was Chaia-- so the gave him Chaim. My second one's Josef Yisrael-- Yosef, after a grandfather of mine, my mother's father. And Yisrael after my father. And the third one, Elli-Moshe. Elli because they fancied it, and Moshe was some-- somebody else in the family.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old are they?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, Pinney's is-- is seven. Yosi, five, coming up for six. And Elli is three, coming up on four.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. And is there anything else before we conclude the interview? Is there anything else?

speaker: interviewee
text: If you want to sit another few hours, I'm sure we can find something else to talk about, but--

speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHING] Is there anything else?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ah, but not enough-- of no importance.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, well thank you very much Mr. Irving. Who is the person in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this picture happens to be of my mother-- which was taken before she was married.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have no idea. Well, this picture-- the back of it-- is written in Yiddish. And it says 1934. But it was taken well before 1934, because 1934, I mean, my mother-- my mother was already a married woman with many-- she had a few children.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who are the people in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, this particular picture is of myself and my younger sister. Now that must have been taken around about 1936, because by that time-- or '37-- by that time, I would have started learning Chumash and [? Gemara. ?] You see, I'm wearing the dress, the coat, of a Hasidic boy that my grandfather gave me when I started learning Chumash.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was taken at Ryki, at home. But it was in the back garden, or front garden-- I don't know. I would imagine it would have been taken in the back garden.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you please describe who is in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this picture, again, must have been, uh, taken the same time as the previous one, because this is myself and my four sisters. And if you see that the dress, of my-- myself and the younger sister, is identical of the previous picture.

speaker: interviewer
text: And could you describe who-- starting from left to right?

speaker: interviewee
text: The oldest-- the oldest one is Rivka. Then next to her, on the right, is Leah. And then on the left is Hinda, and then the youngest one, Mendel. These pictures, by the way, I did not keep during the war. We had no way of keeping anything. When the Germans used to search us from camp to camp, first of all they used to shave us all, they used to look into your body in case you were hiding not only pictures, but jewelry or valuables. So, but these pictures were kept by my uncle in Israel. As children, obviously, my mother used to send him pictures. But unfortunately, I have none of my father or my mother of late. So I wouldn't even know what they look like or anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you please describe this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: Woah [LAUGHS]-- very interesting picture, this. Uh, to me it's worth a lot of money. This picture was taken towards the end of the war by partisans. And this is part of the transport that was explaining that we went from in [NON-ENGLISH]. And if you tune in, uh, on the right, the last little boy on the right, with a beret on, is me. I think you've just-- just about got it in focus. Very difficult.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who's the person in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that is me. And this was just after liberation in Theresienstadt, and I've got the Russian star on my lapel. And this was taken just days after the Russians came in through to the Theresienstadt liberation.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when would that have been?

speaker: interviewee
text: That would have been 1945.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you please describe this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: Uh, which-- [MUMBLES] [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I think it's a group of the '45 Aid Society of [? all the ?] survivors. That was either taken in Theresienstadt or Prague. I would imagine it's in-- in Prague. A group of the-- that we came-- awaiting the transportation to England.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when would that have been taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: Again, '45-- that would have been early August, '45.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is this is a picture of?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of the hostel in Gateshead-- all our friends, all my friends together. Some, unfortunately, are not alive anymore. And this would have been taken, uh-- we arrived in-- could have been taken any time between '45 and '48. Exactly the date, I don't remember. But--

speaker: interviewer
text: Please describe this picture.

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture is actually Windermere, uh, on a trip. On the right here, you've got Israel Cohen, who was one of our leaders. And as part of a group that-- from Windermere, on an outing.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what date would that have been taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it would have been '48-- uh, '45 rather, '45.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you please describe this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this picture would have been-- this is a picture of me in the army. And it was-- would have been around about beginning of May, '49, or thereabout, when I was in a certain camp, you know, I was made into a guard by the gate-- coming into the camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is the person in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: The person in the picture is me. This is taking in 1948, in Marseilles, at the docks, waiting to go to, uh, to Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you please describe what this is a picture of?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, this is the famous boat in Marseilles, called the [NON-ENGLISH], that transported people from Europe to-- to Israel, Palestine.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was in '48. It would have been around about August, September, '48-- August, I would think, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who are the people in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the picture's, uh, of myself and my late wife, on the wedding day in July, 1962. She was beautiful.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taken in, um, this particular picture-- I would imagine it was taken in South Lambtom in the [? Brixton's ?] shul hall-- or shul, or the [? Wentworth's ?] town hall where we have the reception-- South Lambton.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your late wife's name was?

speaker: interviewee
text: My late wife's name was Sandra Ann Silver Irving.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who are the children in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my two daughters. The one on the left, holding the dog, is Hazel-Nechama, and the one on the right is Ruth-Michal.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was Hazel born?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hazel was born the 14th of August, 1963. 63

speaker: interviewer
text: And Ruth?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ruth the 25th of May, 1966.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old were they both in this photo-- in these photos?

speaker: interviewee
text: Um, Hazel must have been four or five-- I would imagine. Ruthie was a baby, still.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where were they taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: And were taken at home, in Greyhound Hill, Hendon.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who are the people in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this is my youngest daughter, Ruth, with her husband, Jeremy Shepson, who were married in, uh, June 28th, 1988.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was this photo taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taken in Jeremy's parents' house, in Finchley.

speaker: interviewer
text: England.

speaker: interviewee
text: In England.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who are the people in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, again, it's yours truly on the right-- [LAUGHS] there's no mistaking that. And Ruth, the middle one, on her wedding day, and Hazel on the left, is my oldest daughter. And this was-- this was taken actually in my house, in Grayhound, before the chuppa.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was, uh, 28th of June in 1988.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you please describe what this picture is?

speaker: interviewee
text: Before I describe this picture, can I please-- I made a terrible mistake. It's July '88, not June. It's July that my Ruth got married. Anyway, this picture, it was taken only a few months ago. It was taken of all my present family, and myself sitting with Jeremy, the three boys, and my two daughters. Now, this picture we were taking when St. Martin Gilbert was writing the book about the boys. He wanted to include pictures of the family to present, recent family pictures. But unfortunately, he didn't-- he didn't have room enough in the book to--

speaker: interviewer
text: And you're--the boys are, from left to right?

speaker: interviewee
text: The boys is-- uh, on the one on the right, is Pinney. The middle one is Yosi, and the end one, on the left, is Elli.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you please describe what this document is?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, fortunate enough-- through, uh, my aunt, Tzipporah, in Israel, she managed-- we've always had the friction, uh, she didn't think I was old enough to remember things from home. And I, uh, persuaded her one year to get me a birth certificate, if it's at all possible. I told her, I didn't want to go into the British embassy-- the Polish embassy in London, because in those early days, after the war, people were watched who goes in and goes out from these communist country embassies. And she managed to get me a copy, in Israel, through a solicitor, of my birth certificate, which states the date I was born, and where, and at my father's name-- which is Icek, it's Srul-Icek. My mother's name was Chana-Necha, and so forth. And this is the certificate I have.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the date is?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know what the date-- the date would have been. I don't know whether they're giving the original date, or what. I'm not quite sure. But that would have been the early '50s.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you please describe what this is?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this is a treasure-- a real treasure. This is a telegram, it was sent the 1st of March, 1940, form [? Rivka, ?] by the Red Cross to my uncle in Palestine, Haifa-- Haifa, Palestine. And there-- there's a message on the back, as well, from my father to say that all was well at home. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This is a real treasure. That's about the only thing I have of my father-- with his writing, especially.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you please describe what these little books are?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, these two little books-- the one on the right, the blue one, is what we call, [NON-ENGLISH], identity card, as you arrived in Israel, as becoming a citizen of the state of Israel. The one on the left is an army book-- in fact, it's the mob book, when I was demobbed-- and that's what they gave me.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did you become a citizen?

speaker: interviewee
text: As soon as you arrive-- whoever arrived, which was in 1948.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when were you demobbed from the army?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was demobbed from the army, I think it was almost May, 1949.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you please describe what these are?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, one-- one is the certificate which I was recently given to identify that I was a volunteer in Israel Army, 1948, 1949. The one on the left at the bottom is the insignia of the army I was in. And that is the insignia of the Palmach-- because during the war, Palmach didn't have insignias at all-- I don't think any of the other armies had, anyway. But this insignia was given in May, '49, before the famous-- the infamous parade that was supposed to have taken place, which didn't.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you please introduce your family for us?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think we'll start off with the two big ones. Hazel, on my left here, Hazel, my oldest daughter. Ruth, on my left again, next to her is Ruth-Michal. And she's holding, or standing next to her is youngest grandson, Elli. And on my right-- first right here is my oldest grandson, Pinney. And here, on my left again, is my middle one-- Yosi. They're all called Shepson.

speaker: interviewer
text: Ruth, do you have a message for your father about-- or anything you'd like to say about your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not publicly. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewee
text: Um, it's very difficult because in all the years that we were growing up, we-- we very rarely spoke about his experiences. And now having read his memoirs that he's written down, and watched interviews that he's made, and having read which I'm getting into quite slowly-- it's all quite fascinating. It's impossible to believe that he went through all of that. And now--

speaker: interviewee
text: And come out the same. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: It's, um, it's quite-- I mean, I know that if I was now on my own with these three-- I thank God I've got a wonderful husband-- I don't know if I could have coped-- if I could cope as he has coped over the years. And how he, you know, he brought us up, and I personally don't think he did a very bad job. He may think otherwise.

speaker: interviewee
text: Did a pretty good job. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewee
text: Um, so, I do-- um, he won't believe me, but I do have a total admiration. And I just-- I find it very difficult to comprehend everything that he's been through, and how he can still be as he is.

speaker: interviewer
text: Michal?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ruth.

speaker: interviewee
text: Hazel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Hazel, sorry.

speaker: interviewee
text: She's Ruth-Michal, I'm Hazel-Nechama.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's all these names.

speaker: interviewee
text: Um, I agree with what Ruthie said. Um, I think that you do become scarred because of what's happened. And it goes through the generations-- you can't help but be affected by it. And it's not the same as someone who has grown up in a background that has not had a Holocaust survivor in the background, or any sort of trauma in the background. But the way Dad's sort of picked up the pieces of that, and everything else that's happened, I think, um, shows-- I think the courage is shown because of what's happened, rather than, um, despite what's happened. He doesn't agree.

speaker: interviewer
text: What message do you have for your grandpa? What do you want to say about your grandpa?

speaker: interviewee
text: Got anything to say about zayde?

speaker: interviewee
text: Start with Pinney.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're thinking. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't have anything to say.

speaker: interviewer
text: Have you got a message to say, then, for your grandpa?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: No?

speaker: interviewee
text: No message to say to zayde? What would you want to tell zayde, if zayde wasn't here? What would you want to tell him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mmm, nothing. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: If he's gone away?

speaker: interviewee
text: If he's gone away. But you can still tell him, you want to say you love him. Yes?

speaker: interviewee
text: What do you want to say?

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: The boys aren't very aware, really-- I mean, they're too young to know what's been going on. But I tell them that, you know, one of the reasons-- and I'm sure this is one of the reasons for daddy coming out and doing this is so that they will know, when they're older, they'll be able to be read, they'll be able to see, they'll be able to understand his history and everything that's happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, whereas as now they're too young to appreciate it, it's not long till they'll be learning bits of history in school, and they can say, yes, our zayde went through that, or we know that from our zayde. Things like that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Come back to me when--

speaker: interviewee
text: You want to say something now? The lady can come back to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: When you come back--

speaker: interviewee
text: When we come back to you, OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Solly, do you want to say something?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, Ruth has touched on it-- and the reason why, uh, I wanted to do this, there's so little that-- however much I've tried to tell them, speak about it, it's very difficult to absorb it all. And I'm doing this for my children, grandchildren, that when they have time, an occasion to look through this video, to be able to grasp a little bit of the background that I come from. I can't tell them much about my own parents-- maybe through the video, they'll be able to grasp a little bit of it. They were too young, that when the family was alive, to ask them about it. And the family weren't ready forthcoming. Uh, and that's just sad-- it's difficult to be able to explain to them in detail, exactly. And hopefully that when they have, that they will be able to look through the video and see a little bit, learn a little bit, and hope and pray that they will never have to come through a period alive-- that [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewee
text: Quickly, because the tape's going to run out.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about you two? You don't want to say, I love you, grandpa? Nothing?

speaker: interviewee
text: I love you, grandpa.

speaker: interviewee
text: Grandpa-- say, you have to say zayde. You have to say zayde.

speaker: interviewee
text: I love you, zayde.

speaker: interviewee
text: What, you're giving mommy a hug? Not grandpa? [INTERPOSING VOICES]

speaker: interviewee
text: They got camera shy. [INTERPOSING VOICES]

speaker: interviewee
text: I love you.

speaker: interviewee
text: What about you, Yosi?

speaker: interviewee
text: They don't need to say it on tape. It's [INAUDIBLE]. [INTERPOSING VOICES]

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.
